title: Everyday English in Real Life
description: Communicate naturally in everyday situations — social conversations, phone calls, requests, opinions, complaints, and digital communication.
featured_dialogues: Scenario 1: Friends catching up at a café (L1)

lessons.title: Question tags — …isn't it? …aren't you?
lessons.description: Learn to use question tags to check information, invite agreement, and sound natural in conversation.

content.title: What are question tags?
content.body:
A QUESTION TAG is a short question added to the end of a statement. We use them to:
• Check information we think is true
• Invite the other person to agree
• Start or keep a conversation going

The rule:
POSITIVE statement → NEGATIVE tag
'It's a beautiful day, isn't it?'

NEGATIVE statement → POSITIVE tag
'You're not from here, are you?'

More examples:
• 'She works in IT, doesn't she?'
• 'They didn't come, did they?'
• 'You've been to Goa, haven't you?'
• 'He's very talented, isn't he?'
content.grammar_note:
Tag = auxiliary verb + pronoun
Positive → negative tag: is → isn't, do → don't, have → haven't
Negative → positive tag: isn't → is, don't → do

content.title: Question tags — tense matching
content.body:
The tag verb must MATCH the main verb tense:

Present simple:
• 'She works here, doesn't she?'
• 'They live in Mumbai, don't they?'

Present continuous:
• 'You're studying for the exam, aren't you?'

Past simple:
• 'He called you, didn't he?'
• 'They didn't come, did they?'

Present perfect:
• 'You've met her before, haven't you?'

Modal verbs:
• 'She can swim, can't she?'
• 'You'll be there, won't you?'
• 'They should know, shouldn't they?'

Special case — 'I am':
• 'I'm next, aren't I?' (NOT 'amn't I')
content.grammar_note:
Match the tense of the main clause
Use the SAME auxiliary in the tag
I am → aren't I (special case)

vocabulary.meaning: to check that something is correct
to ask someone to say they agree
to make sure something is true
vocabulary.example: The train leaves at 6, doesn't it?

vocabulary.meaning: a short question added to a statement
vocabulary.example: 'It's lovely here, isn't it?' — 'isn't it' is the tag.

vocabulary.meaning: the rise and fall of your voice
vocabulary.example: Rising intonation = genuine question. Falling = seeking agreement.

vocabulary.meaning: asking because you truly don't know
vocabulary.example: You haven't met him, have you? (I'm not sure)

sentences: It's a beautiful evening, isn't it?
You work at TCS, don't you?
She hasn't called back yet, has she?
They were at the concert, weren't they?
You can drive, can't you?

content.question: Choose the correct question tag: 'Ravi is your brother, ___?'
content.correct: isn't he
content.explanation: Positive statement (is) → negative tag (isn't). Subject = he (Ravi).

content.question: Choose the correct tag: 'She didn't attend the meeting, ___?'
content.correct: did she
content.explanation: Negative statement (didn't) → positive tag (did). Subject = she.

content.question: Choose the correct tag: 'You've been to Kerala, ___?'
content.correct: haven't you
content.explanation: Present perfect positive (have been) → negative tag (haven't). Subject = you.

content.question: Which sentence has the CORRECT question tag?
options: They don't know the answer, do they?
She is coming tomorrow, is she?
We went there, didn't we not?
content.correct: They don't know the answer, do they?
content.explanation: Negative (don't) → positive tag (do). 'They' → 'they'. Correct!

content.instruction: Add the correct question tag.

sentences.text: The food here is really good, ___ ___?
You didn't finish your chai, ___ ___?
Meera works in marketing, ___ ___?
They've already left, ___ ___?
You'll be at the party tonight, ___ ___?

content.instruction: Complete the question tag — match the tense.

sentences.text: She was at the conference last week, ___ ___?
You can speak Tamil, ___ ___?
He shouldn't do that, ___ ___?
I'm the last one, ___ ___?

content.instruction: Arrange into a complete sentence with a question tag.
content.answer: It's a great movie, isn't it?

content.instruction: Arrange into a sentence with a negative statement + positive tag.
content.answer: You haven't met Ravi before, have you?

content.instruction: Match each statement to the correct question tag.

pairs.right: doesn't she?
did they?
haven't you?
will he?
aren't I?

content.title: ★ FEATURED DIALOGUE — Scenario 1: Friends catching up at a café — Chennai
content.setting: Priya and Kavya are old college friends who haven't met in a year. They meet at a café in Chennai. This dialogue is full of question tags used naturally in conversation.
content.learning_note: Listen for how question tags are used to CHECK information, INVITE AGREEMENT, and KEEP THE CONVERSATION GOING. You will practise this dialogue again in Lesson 13.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Kavya! Oh my goodness — it's been so long, hasn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I know! Almost a year, isn't it? You look wonderful, by the way.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Thank you! You haven't changed at all, have you? Still the same Kavya!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! Well, a lot has changed actually. You know I moved to Bangalore, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes! You're working at that tech company, aren't you? TechNova or something?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: TechBridge — close enough! It's going really well. It's a bit hectic though, isn't it always?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Ha, yes. You were always a hard worker, weren't you? Even in college.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: You weren't so bad yourself! You're still teaching, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes, still at the same school. Different class this year though. You haven't tried this café before, have you?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: No — first time! The filter coffee here is amazing, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: The best in Adyar, I think. Your family is still in Coimbatore, aren't they?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, my parents are. But my sister got married and moved to Dubai — you remember her, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course! She was in the year below us, wasn't she? How lovely! We should catch up properly — lunch next time, shouldn't we?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Absolutely. It's long overdue, isn't it? I'll be back in Chennai next month — we'll plan something then!

content.repeat_speaking_note: In Lesson 13, you will: (1) listen and repeat this dialogue, (2) answer comprehension questions, (3) identify all question tags used, and (4) role-play it with a partner.

comprehension_questions.question: How long has it been since Priya and Kavya met?
Where did Kavya's sister move after getting married?

discussion_questions: Find all the question tags in the dialogue. How many are there?
Which tags are used to CHECK information vs INVITE AGREEMENT?
Which response do you find most natural? Why?

content.instruction: Find and correct the question tag mistake.

sentences.wrong: She works in the hospital, isn't she?
sentences.correct: She works in the hospital, doesn't she?
sentences.explanation: Simple present 'works' → tag uses DO/DOES: 'doesn't she'. 'Isn't' is for the verb TO BE.

sentences.wrong: You've been to Mysore, have you?
sentences.correct: You've been to Mysore, haven't you?
sentences.explanation: Positive statement (have been) → NEGATIVE tag (haven't you).

content.scenario: You meet someone at a friend's wedding. You think you've seen them at the gym but aren't 100% sure. What do you say to start a conversation?
options: Do you go to the gym?
You go to the gym, don't you? I think I've seen you there.
I saw you at the gym, didn't I not?
content.correct: You go to the gym, don't you? I think I've seen you there.
content.explanation: A question tag is perfect here — you're not 100% sure, so you're checking while starting a conversation naturally.

content.instruction: Use question tags to have a short conversation. Imagine you've just met someone at a friend's gathering. Use question tags to check things you think are true about them.
content.prompt: Create 6 sentences using question tags in a natural conversation starter. Use different tenses.
content.example_response: Nice to meet you! You're Meera's colleague, aren't you? I thought I recognised you from her photos. You work in the design team, don't you? I remember her mentioning someone from Hyderabad. You're originally from Hyderabad, aren't you? It must be a big change coming to Chennai — the weather's different, isn't it? Much more humid! You've been here a few months, haven't you? You'll get used to the summers eventually — everyone does, don't they? Ha! Anyway, it's lovely to meet you — Meera's mentioned you a lot, hasn't she? Only good things, I promise!

content.instruction: Read the Scenario 1 dialogue again and answer these questions in your own words.
content.prompt:
Answer these questions based on the Café Scenario dialogue:
1. Why are Priya and Kavya meeting?
2. What has changed in Kavya's life since they last met?
3. What question tags do you notice in the conversation? Give 3 examples.
4. How do they plan to meet next time?
content.example_response: Priya and Kavya are meeting because they haven't seen each other for almost a year — they're old college friends who have stayed in touch but haven't had a chance to sit down and catch up properly. A lot has changed in Kavya's life since they last met. She has moved to Bangalore and started working at a company called TechBridge. Her sister also got married and moved to Dubai. As for question tags — there are many! For example: 'it's been so long, hasn't it?' — checking if they agree it's been a long time. 'You're working at TechNova, aren't you?' — Priya is checking what she thinks she knows but isn't sure about. 'You haven't tried this café before, have you?' — a genuine check because Kavya just arrived. They plan to meet for lunch next time Kavya is in Chennai, which will be next month.

content.instruction: Complete this short conversation using correct question tags.
content.text:
Arjun: You went to the conference in Delhi last week, ___ (1)?
Meera: Yes I did! It was great. You've been there before, ___ (2)?
Arjun: A couple of times, yes. The venue is always impressive, ___ (3)?
Meera: Absolutely. The keynote speaker was brilliant — you watched the recording, ___ (4)?
Arjun: Not yet — I should, ___ (5)? You'll send me the link, ___ (6)?

content.title: Lesson 1 complete — Question tags
key_points: Positive statement → NEGATIVE tag
Negative statement → POSITIVE tag
Tag = auxiliary + pronoun — must match the main verb tense
I am → aren't I (special case)
Rising intonation = genuine question; Falling intonation = seeking agreement
Use tags to check info, invite agreement, and keep conversation going
content.next_lesson: So do I / Neither do I — agreeing and disagreeing

lessons.title: So do I / Neither do I — agreeing in conversation
lessons.description: Use 'so' and 'neither/nor' to agree with positive and negative statements naturally in conversation.

content.title: Agreeing with positive statements — So + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something POSITIVE and you agree — you feel the same:

Use: SO + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
• 'I love filter coffee.' → 'So do I!'
• 'I'm really tired today.' → 'So am I!'
• 'I was at the match last night.' → 'So was I!'
• 'I've been to Goa.' → 'So have I!'
• 'I'll be at the meeting.' → 'So will I!'
• 'I can speak Telugu.' → 'So can I!'

The auxiliary verb MATCHES the original statement.
content.grammar_note:
SO + auxiliary + I/we/she/he/they
Match the tense and modal: love→do, am→am, was→was, can→can

content.title: Agreeing with negative statements — Neither/Nor + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something NEGATIVE and you feel the same:

Use: NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
• 'I don't like spicy food.' → 'Neither do I.' / 'Nor do I.'
• 'I'm not ready yet.' → 'Neither am I.'
• 'I haven't been to the North-East.' → 'Neither have I.'
• 'I can't swim.' → 'Neither can I.'
• 'I won't be there.' → 'Neither will I.'

NEITHER and NOR mean the same here — both are correct.
Alternative: 'Me neither.' (informal, very common in speech)
content.grammar_note:
NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary + I/she/he/they
'Me neither' = informal alternative
Word order: Neither do I (NOT Neither I do)

content.title: Disagreeing — expressing a different view
content.body:
When you DON'T agree (you feel differently):

Use: Actually, I do. / Actually, I don't.

Examples:
• 'I love cricket.' → 'Really? I don't actually. I prefer football.'
• 'I don't like the heat.' → 'Oh, I do! I love hot weather.'
• 'I'm not worried about the exam.' → 'Oh, I am! I'm really nervous.'

Soften disagreement with:
• 'Really? I actually...' 
• 'That's interesting — I feel quite differently.'
• 'Oh, I'm the opposite — I...'

Never just say: 'No, wrong!' — always soften it.
content.grammar_note:
Disagreeing: 'Oh, I do/don't actually'
'Really? I'm the opposite — I...'
Always sound warm, not confrontational

sentences: I really enjoy long train journeys. — So do I! There's something relaxing about them.
I'm a bit nervous about the presentation. — So am I. Let's practise together.
I haven't tried Chettinad food yet. — Neither have I — we should go!
I can't stand traffic in the evening. — Neither can I. It's exhausting.
I love early mornings. — Really? I don't actually. I'm a night person!

content.question: Priya says: 'I'm really stressed about the deadline.' You feel the same. What do you say?
content.correct: So am I.
content.explanation: Positive statement with 'am' → So + AM + I. 'So am I' = I am also stressed.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I don't drink coffee at all.' You also don't drink coffee. What do you say?
content.correct: Neither do I.
content.explanation: Negative statement (don't) → NEITHER + DO + I. Word order: Neither + aux + subject.

content.question: Kavya says: 'I've never been to a cricket match.' Ravi has been to cricket matches. What does he say?
options: Really? I have! It's amazing — you must go.
content.correct: Really? I have! It's amazing — you must go.
content.explanation: Ravi DISAGREES — he HAS been. So he says 'Really? I have!' not 'So/Neither have I'.

content.question: Meera says: 'I can't cook at all!' Sonal also can't cook. What does Sonal say?
options: Neither can I — I survive on Swiggy!
content.correct: Neither can I — I survive on Swiggy!
content.explanation: Negative (can't) → NEITHER + CAN + I. The added joke makes it sound natural!

content.instruction: Complete the agreement response using So or Neither + the correct auxiliary.

sentences.text: 'I love Bollywood movies.' → '___ ___ ___!'
'I was exhausted after the trip.' → '___ ___ ___.'
'I haven't finished the report.' → '___ ___ ___.'
'I won't be in the office tomorrow.' → '___ ___ ___.'
'I'm really enjoying this course.' → '___ ___ ___!'

content.instruction: Write the DISAGREEMENT response naturally.

sentences.text: 'I hate spicy food.' → 'Really? ___ ___! I love it — the spicier the better.'
'I'm really good at cooking.' → 'Oh, ___ ___! I can barely boil water.'
'I didn't enjoy the movie.' → 'Really? ___ ___ — I thought it was brilliant!'

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct agreement response.
content.answer: So was I — what a beautiful day.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct negative agreement.
content.answer: Neither have I been to Kerala.

content.instruction: Match the statement to the correct agreement response.

pairs.right: So do I — no commute!
Neither am I. I need two coffees before I can function.

pairs.left: I've visited all the southern states.
pairs.right: So have I! Except Telangana — I still need to go.
Neither can I. I get nightmares!
So will I — we should grab lunch at least!

content.title: ★ FEATURED DIALOGUE — Scenario 2: Office colleagues agreeing and disagreeing — Bangalore
content.setting: Three colleagues — Ravi, Sonal, and Arjun — are having chai in the break room and chatting about work and life. This dialogue features So/Neither structures and natural agreement/disagreement.
content.learning_note: Notice how So do I / Neither do I / Me neither / Oh, I do! create natural flow. This dialogue is revisited in Lesson 13 for speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I've been really struggling with the new project management software. It's so complicated!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So have I! Nobody explained it properly, did they?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I actually find it quite intuitive. Maybe I can walk you both through it?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: That would be great. I also haven't taken my annual leave yet this year.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither have I! I keep saying I'll plan a trip and then something comes up.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Me neither, honestly. I haven't even looked at my leave balance in months.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I really need a holiday. I'm completely burnt out.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So am I. The last two months have been non-stop.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I don't feel too bad actually — I've been sleeping much better since I stopped working late.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh, I can't do that. I'm a night owl — I do my best work after 10 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: So do I! I can't concentrate during the day with all the noise.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I'm the opposite. I can't work at night at all. But we all get results in the end, don't we?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: True! By the way — do you both like the new canteen menu?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I love it! So much better than before.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: So do I — but I wish they had more vegetarian options.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither do I mind vegetarian — I've been trying to eat less meat recently anyway!

comprehension_questions.question: Who finds the new project management software easy to use?
Who works best late at night?
What does Arjun wish the canteen had more of?

discussion_questions: Count how many times 'So + aux + I' is used vs 'Neither + aux + I'.
Find two examples of disagreement. How do the speakers soften their disagreement?
What do all three colleagues have in common? What is different?

content.instruction: Find and correct the mistake.

sentences.explanation: Word order: SO + auxiliary + subject. 'So do I' — not 'So I do'.

sentences.wrong: 'I haven't seen that film.' → 'Neither I have.'
sentences.correct: 'I haven't seen that film.' → 'Neither have I.'
sentences.explanation: Word order: NEITHER + auxiliary + subject. 'Neither have I' — not 'Neither I have'.
NEITHER already contains the negative meaning — don't add 'not' again.

content.scenario: Your friend says: 'I've never tried sushi.' You also haven't tried sushi. What's the most natural response?
options: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.correct: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.explanation: NEITHER HAVE I is the correct structure. Adding a follow-up suggestion makes it feel like natural conversation.

content.instruction: Play the agreement game — respond to each statement below using So/Neither + the correct auxiliary. Then add a natural follow-up comment.
content.prompt:
Respond to these 5 statements with So/Neither + auxiliary. Then add one extra sentence each time:
1. 'I love street food.'
2. 'I'm not a fan of Mondays.'
3. 'I've been learning a new skill recently.'
4. 'I can't imagine living without my phone.'
5. 'I didn't sleep well last night.'
content.example_response: Let me respond to all five! First: 'I love street food' — So do I! There's a great pani puri stall near my office that I visit almost every evening. Second: 'I'm not a fan of Mondays' — Neither am I! Mondays always feel like such a slow start. I need at least two coffees to get going. Third: 'I've been learning a new skill recently' — So have I! I've been taking online classes for data visualisation — it's challenging but really interesting. Fourth: 'I can't imagine living without my phone' — Neither can I honestly. Everything is on there — work, family, navigation, entertainment. Fifth: 'I didn't sleep well last night' — Neither did I! I was lying awake thinking about the presentation today. Glad it went well in the end!

content.instruction: Read the Scenario 2 dialogue and answer these questions in your own words.
content.prompt:
Answer based on the office break room dialogue:
1. What do Ravi and Sonal have in common regarding software?
2. Why haven't any of them taken their annual leave?
3. What are Ravi and Sonal's working style — and how is Arjun's different?
4. What opinion do the three colleagues share about the new canteen?
content.example_response: In the office dialogue, Ravi and Sonal have something in common — they're both struggling with the new project management software. Neither of them found it easy to learn because it wasn't properly explained to them. Arjun, interestingly, is the exception — he finds it quite intuitive and even offers to help them. As for annual leave, none of them have taken it yet this year. They all keep planning to go on holiday but something always comes up and they end up postponing it. About working style — Ravi and Sonal are both night owls. They do their best work late at night and find it hard to concentrate during the day with office noise. Arjun is the opposite — he can't work at night at all and goes to sleep early. Regarding the canteen, all three seem to like the new menu. Ravi loves it and thinks it's much better than before. Arjun agrees but wishes there were more vegetarian options.

content.instruction: Complete this conversation using So, Neither, or a disagreement response.
content.text:
Priya: I've been exercising every day this week.
Ravi: ___ (1) ___ ___! I started a new jogging routine on Monday.
Priya: I also haven't eaten any junk food.
Ravi: ___ (2) ___ ___. Well — I did have one samosa on Tuesday. Okay maybe two.
Priya: Ha! I'm really feeling the difference though.
Ravi: ___ (3) ___ ___! More energy, better sleep.
Priya: I can't believe I didn't start this sooner.
Ravi: ___ (4) — I always kept saying 'I'll start on Monday'!
Priya: I won't give up this time though.
Ravi: ___ (5) ___ ___! Let's keep each other accountable.

content.title: Lesson 2 complete — So do I / Neither do I
key_points: Agree with POSITIVE: SO + auxiliary + I (So do I / So am I / So have I)
Agree with NEGATIVE: NEITHER + auxiliary + I (Neither do I / Neither am I)
Word order: SO/NEITHER + auxiliary + subject (NOT So I do)
Informal: 'Me too' (positive) / 'Me neither' (negative)
Disagree politely: 'Really? I do / I don't actually...'
The auxiliary must MATCH the tense of the original statement
content.next_lesson: Indirect questions — Could you tell me where…?

lessons.title: Indirect questions — Could you tell me where…?
lessons.description: Ask questions more politely and formally using indirect question structures.

content.title: What is an indirect question?
content.body:
Direct questions are short and clear. Indirect questions are more POLITE — we use them with strangers, in formal situations, or when we want to sound less abrupt.

DIRECT → INDIRECT:
• 'Where is the station?' → 'Could you tell me where the station is?'
• 'What time does it close?' → 'Do you know what time it closes?'
• 'Is there a pharmacy nearby?' → 'Could you tell me if there's a pharmacy nearby?'
• 'When does the bus leave?' → 'Do you have any idea when the bus leaves?'

Key change: The INDIRECT question uses STATEMENT word order — NOT question word order.
Direct: 'Where IS the station?' (inverted)
Indirect: '...where the station IS.' (normal)
content.grammar_note:
Indirect questions = statement word order inside
NO inversion after the indirect opener
NO auxiliary DO/DID in indirect wh- questions

content.title: Indirect question openers + if/whether
content.body:
Common indirect question OPENERS:
• Could you tell me…?
• Do you know…?
• Do you have any idea…?
• Would you mind telling me…?
• I was wondering if…
• Can you tell me…?

For YES/NO questions — use IF or WHETHER:
Direct: 'Is the meeting on Friday?'
Indirect: 'Could you tell me IF/WHETHER the meeting is on Friday?'

Direct: 'Does she speak English?'
Indirect: 'Do you know IF she speaks English?'

WH- questions — use the same wh- word:
Direct: 'Where is the manager?'
Indirect: 'Could you tell me WHERE the manager is?'
content.grammar_note:
YES/NO → indirect opener + IF/WHETHER
WH- → indirect opener + wh- word
Statement word order always follows the opener

content.title: Vocabulary: Polite requests and service language

vocabulary.example: I'm calling to enquire about the job vacancy.
Please don't hesitate to ask if you need help.
Could you clarify what the process involves?
I need some assistance with this form.
The direct question was too blunt for the situation.
An indirect question sounds more polite.

sentences: Could you tell me where the nearest ATM is?
Do you know if the office is open on Sundays?
I was wondering whether you could help me with this form.
Would you mind telling me what time the last train leaves?
Do you have any idea how long the wait will be?

content.question: Convert to indirect: 'Where is the post office?' Choose the correct version.
options: Could you tell me where is the post office?
Could you tell me where the post office is?
Could you tell me where does the post office is?
Do you know where is post office?
content.correct: Could you tell me where the post office is?
content.explanation: Indirect questions use statement word order: 'where the post office IS' (not 'where IS the post office').

content.question: Convert: 'Is there parking near the office?' → Choose the best indirect version.
options: Could you tell me there is parking near the office?
Could you tell me if there is parking near the office?
Do you know is there parking near the office?
I was wondering does there parking near the office?
content.correct: Could you tell me if there is parking near the office?
content.explanation: YES/NO question → use IF after the indirect opener. Word order stays normal.

content.question: Which situation is MOST appropriate for an indirect question?
options: Asking a close friend where they bought their shoes.
Asking a senior manager in a formal meeting when the results will be shared.
Shouting across the room to a colleague.
Texting a friend about weekend plans.
content.correct: Asking a senior manager in a formal meeting when the results will be shared.
content.explanation: Indirect questions are for formal or polite situations — with strangers, managers, or in professional contexts.

content.question: Which indirect question is CORRECT?
options: I was wondering where does the bus stop.
Do you know when will the results arrive?
Could you tell me how much the ticket costs?
Would you mind to tell me the way?
content.correct: Could you tell me how much the ticket costs?
content.explanation: 'How much the ticket costs' = statement order (no inversion, no extra 'does'). Correct!

content.instruction: Convert the direct question to an indirect question.

sentences.text: 'What time does the bank open?' → 'Could you tell me what time ___?'
'Is the manager available?' → 'Do you know ___ the manager is available?'
'How do I get to Platform 4?' → 'Could you tell me ___ I get to Platform 4?'
'Did she receive the email?' → 'Do you know ___ she received the email?'

content.instruction: Complete the indirect question with the correct word order.

sentences.text: Could you tell me where ___ the nearest hospital? (is)
Do you know if ___ on weekends? (the office/open)
I was wondering whether ___ any student discounts. (there are)
Would you mind telling me how long ___ from here? (it takes)

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct indirect question.
content.answer: Could you tell me what time the mall closes?

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct indirect YES/NO question.
content.answer: Do you know if the conference room is available?

content.instruction: Match each direct question to its correct indirect version.

pairs.right: Could you tell me where the lift is?
Do you know if there's a vegetarian menu?

pairs.left: How long does the tour take?
pairs.right: Do you have any idea how long the tour takes?
I was wondering what the Wi-Fi password is.
Could you tell me whether the price includes breakfast?

content.title: Indirect questions in a professional setting — hospital reception, Hyderabad
content.setting: Kavya is visiting a hospital for the first time to take her elderly neighbour. She uses indirect questions throughout to be polite.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Excuse me — could you tell me where the general outpatient department is?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Of course! It's on the second floor — take the lift on your left.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Thank you. Do you know if we need to take a token number first?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Yes, you do. The token counter is right here at reception. Do you have an appointment?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Not exactly — it's a walk-in. Could you tell me how long the waiting time usually is?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: It depends on the day — today is about 40 to 50 minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: I see. Do you have any idea whether there's a place to sit near the token counter?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Yes — there's a waiting area just around the corner with seating for about 30 people.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: That's helpful. I was also wondering if there's a pharmacy inside the hospital.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Yes, on the ground floor near the exit. It's open until 9 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Perfect. Could you also tell me if they accept health insurance cards here?

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.text: Yes — most major insurance providers are accepted. The billing desk on the first floor can help you with that.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Thank you so much — you've been very helpful!

comprehension_questions.question: Where is the general outpatient department?
Approximately how long is the wait today?

discussion_questions: Count the indirect questions Kavya uses. How many are wh- questions? How many are if/whether?
How would this conversation sound if Kavya used direct questions instead? Would it feel rude?
Why is the indirect form more appropriate in a hospital setting?

content.instruction: Find and correct the indirect question mistake.

sentences.wrong: Could you tell me where is the manager?
sentences.correct: Could you tell me where the manager is?
sentences.explanation: Indirect questions use statement word order — no inversion. 'where the manager is' not 'where is the manager'.

sentences.wrong: Do you know when does the meeting start?
sentences.correct: Do you know when the meeting starts?
sentences.explanation: No auxiliary DO/DOES in indirect wh- questions. 'when the meeting starts' not 'when does it start'.

sentences.wrong: I was wondering if is there a vegetarian option?
sentences.correct: I was wondering if there is a vegetarian option.
sentences.explanation: After IF in an indirect question, use statement order: 'if there is' not 'if is there'.

content.scenario: You are at a railway station in a new city. You need to find out which platform the Shatabdi Express leaves from. A station officer is standing nearby. What do you say?
options: Could you tell me which platform the Shatabdi Express departs from?
content.correct: Could you tell me which platform the Shatabdi Express departs from?
content.explanation: Polite, formal situation → indirect question. Correct word order: 'which platform the Shatabdi Express departs from'.

content.instruction: You are a tourist in a new city in India. You need to find out 5 things. Ask each question as an INDIRECT question to a local person.
content.prompt: Ask 5 indirect questions about: (1) the nearest metro station, (2) whether the museum is open today, (3) how long it takes to reach the beach, (4) where you can find good local food, (5) whether taxis are available nearby.
content.example_response: Excuse me — I hope you don't mind me asking! First — could you tell me where the nearest metro station is? I've been walking for a while and I can't seem to find it. Second — do you know if the city museum is open today? I read it might be closed on Mondays. Third — I was wondering how long it takes to reach the beach from here on foot or by auto. Fourth — could you tell me where I could find some good local food? I want to try authentic food from this area, not just the big restaurants. And finally — do you know if taxis or autos are easily available at this time of day, or should I book one online? Thank you so much — I really appreciate your help!

content.instruction: Think about a time you needed help in an unfamiliar place — a new city, hospital, government office, airport. How did you ask for help? Recount the experience using indirect questions.
content.prompt: Describe a real or imagined experience where you used or should have used indirect questions to ask for information politely.
content.example_response: When I first moved to Bangalore from my hometown, everything was new and a little overwhelming. I had to visit the Regional Passport Office for the first time and I was very nervous. I didn't know the process at all. When I arrived, I approached the security officer and I said: 'Excuse me, could you tell me where the token counter is?' He directed me inside. Then I asked the person at the counter: 'I was wondering whether I need to submit all original documents today or whether photocopies are sufficient?' She said originals were needed for verification only. I then asked her: 'Do you know approximately how long the process takes for a first-time applicant?' She said around two to three hours. Later I had to ask: 'Could you tell me if there's a place to have lunch nearby?' because I hadn't eaten. Using indirect questions made me feel more confident — people responded very warmly when I asked politely.
key_phrases: Do you have any idea how long...?

content.instruction: Complete the indirect questions in this customer enquiry call.
content.text:
Customer: Good morning. I'm calling to enquire about a product. Could you tell me ___ (1) the BX500 model is still available?

Staff: Yes, it is! We have it in stock.

Customer: Wonderful. Do you know ___ (2) it costs?

Staff: The BX500 is priced at ₹14,999.

Customer: I see. Could you also tell me ___ (3) it comes with a warranty?

Staff: Yes — a two-year manufacturer's warranty is included.

Customer: Great. I was wondering ___ (4) you offer EMI options.

Staff: We do! We have 6-month and 12-month no-cost EMI available.

Customer: Perfect. And do you have any idea ___ (5) delivery would take if I order today?

Staff: It would arrive within 3 to 5 business days.

content.title: Lesson 3 complete — Indirect questions
key_points: Indirect questions are MORE POLITE — use with strangers, in formal situations
Common openers: Could you tell me…? / Do you know…? / I was wondering…?
WH- questions → use same wh- word + statement word order
YES/NO questions → use IF or WHETHER after the opener
NO inversion, NO extra do/does/did in indirect questions
Statement word order: 'where the station IS' (not 'where IS the station')
content.next_lesson: Polite requests — would you mind / could you possibly

lessons.title: Polite requests — Would you mind / Could you possibly
lessons.description: Make polite requests in formal and everyday situations using a range of structures from gentle to very polite.

content.title: A scale of politeness for requests
content.body:
Requests in English range from casual to very formal. Choose based on the situation:

MOST CASUAL (friends, family):
• 'Give me that pen.' / 'Pass the salt.'
• 'Can you help me?'

POLITE (colleagues, shopkeepers, most situations):
• 'Could you help me, please?'
• 'Would you help me?'

MORE POLITE (strangers, bosses, formal):
• 'Could you possibly help me?'
• 'Would it be possible to...?'

MOST POLITE (formal or when asking a big favour):
• 'Would you mind helping me?'
• 'I was wondering if you could possibly...'
• 'Would you be so kind as to...?'
content.grammar_note:
COULD = more polite than CAN
WOULD YOU MIND + -ING (gerund)
'Possibly' makes any request softer

content.title: Would you mind…? — special structure
content.body:
WOULD YOU MIND is followed by a GERUND (-ing form):

• 'Would you mind opening the window?'
• 'Would you mind waiting for a moment?'
• 'Would you mind repeating that?'
• 'Would you mind sending me the document?'

Answering 'Would you mind...?':
✓ 'Not at all!' (= I don't mind = Yes, I'll do it)
✓ 'Of course!' (= happy to)
✓ 'No problem.'
✓ 'Sure!'
✗ 'No.' (this sounds like refusal — confusing!)

Note: 'No, not at all' = I'm happy to help (confusing but common)
content.grammar_note:
WOULD YOU MIND + verb-ING
'Not at all' = positive response (I'll do it)
Don't say just 'No' — it sounds like refusal

vocabulary.meaning: something you do to help someone
vocabulary.example: Could you do me a favour?

vocabulary.meaning: to do something as a favour

vocabulary.example: I hope this isn't inconvenient for you.

vocabulary.meaning: to give a small amount of something

vocabulary.example: Could you assist me with this form?

sentences: Could you possibly send me the file by end of day?
Would you mind moving your bag? I'd like to sit down.
I was wondering if you could help me with this form.
Would it be possible to change my appointment to Thursday?
Not at all — I'd be happy to help!

content.question: Which is the most polite way to ask a senior colleague to review your report?
options: Can you look at my report?
I was wondering if you could possibly take a look at my report when you have a moment.
Look at this for me please.
content.correct: I was wondering if you could possibly take a look at my report when you have a moment.
content.explanation: 'I was wondering if you could possibly...' + 'when you have a moment' = very polite, acknowledges their time.

content.question: 'Would you mind closing the door?' — What is the correct positive response?
content.correct: Not at all — of course!
content.explanation: 'Not at all' = I don't mind = Yes, I'll do it. Just saying 'No' sounds like a refusal!

content.question: Which sentence uses 'Would you mind' CORRECTLY?
content.correct: Would you mind waiting?
content.explanation: WOULD YOU MIND + verb-ING (gerund). 'Waiting' not 'to wait' or 'wait'.

content.question: You need to use your office phone quickly. Your colleague is on a long personal call. What do you say politely?
options: Excuse me — would you mind if I used the phone quickly? It's a brief work call.
Can I have the phone now?
content.correct: Excuse me — would you mind if I used the phone quickly? It's a brief work call.
content.explanation: Polite request + brief explanation = natural and considerate. 'Would you mind if I...' is very polite.

content.instruction: Complete the polite request using the given structure.

sentences.text: ___ you mind ___ (open) the window? It's quite warm.
Could you ___ send me the agenda before the meeting?
I was ___ if you could spare a few minutes to help me.
Would it be ___ to reschedule for next week?
I'd really ___ your help with this task.

content.instruction: Write a more polite version of each direct request.

sentences.text: 'Turn the music down.' → '___ you mind ___ the music down?'
'Explain that again.' → 'Could you possibly ___ that again?'
'Help me.' → 'I was wondering if you ___ ___ me.'
'Move your chair.' → 'Would it be possible for you to ___ your chair slightly?'

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct polite request.
content.answer: I was wondering if you could send the file.

content.instruction: Arrange into a 'Would you mind' request.
content.answer: Would you mind checking the report again?

content.instruction: Match the situation to the most appropriate polite request.

pairs.left: Asking a friend to take a photo
pairs.right: Could you take a photo of us?

pairs.left: Asking a manager for an extension
pairs.right: I was wondering if it would be possible to have a brief extension.

pairs.left: Asking a stranger to move their bag on a bus
pairs.right: Would you mind moving your bag? I'd like to sit down.

pairs.left: Asking a colleague to lower their voice
pairs.right: Could you possibly speak a little more softly?
Would it be possible to get the leave request form from you?

content.title: ★ FEATURED DIALOGUE — Scenario 3: Customer service call — Priya calls a bank, Chennai
content.setting: Priya's debit card was blocked and she needs to get it unblocked. She calls her bank's customer service line. This dialogue features polite requests throughout.
content.learning_note: Notice how BOTH the customer and the bank agent use polite language. This scenario is revisited in Lesson 13 for speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Good afternoon, City Bank customer service. How may I assist you today?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. I'm calling because my debit card seems to have been blocked. I'm not sure why.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: I'm sorry to hear that. Could you please verify your name and registered mobile number?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Of course — my name is Priya Rajan and the number is 98765 43210.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Thank you. And could you tell me your account number or the last 4 digits of your card?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: The last 4 digits are 7824.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Perfect. Would you mind holding for a moment while I pull up your account?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Not at all, please go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Thank you for holding, Ms Rajan. I can see your card was blocked after 3 incorrect PIN attempts. Is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Oh — yes, I think I typed the wrong PIN at the petrol station. Could you possibly unblock it for me?

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Absolutely. Would you mind confirming your date of birth for security purposes?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Yes — 14th March 1992.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Perfect. Your card has been unblocked now. Could I ask you to try a transaction and confirm it works?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: I will — thank you so much! One more thing — would it be possible to also increase my daily transaction limit?

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: Of course — I can raise it up to ₹1 lakh per day. Would that work for you?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That would be perfect. I really appreciate your help today.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.text: My pleasure, Ms Rajan. Is there anything else I can help you with?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: No, that's everything. Thank you very much! Bye!

comprehension_questions.question: Why was Priya's card blocked?
options: She entered the wrong PIN 3 times.
comprehension_questions.correct: She entered the wrong PIN 3 times.

comprehension_questions.question: What did Priya ask for in addition to unblocking?
options: An increase in her daily transaction limit
comprehension_questions.correct: An increase in her daily transaction limit

comprehension_questions.question: What did the agent ask Priya to confirm for security?

discussion_questions: Find 4 polite requests in the dialogue — who makes them and what structure is used?
Why does the agent also use polite language even though they are the service provider?
How does Priya's response 'Not at all, please go ahead' show politeness?

content.instruction: Find and correct the polite request mistake.

sentences.wrong: Would you mind to send the report by tomorrow?
sentences.correct: Would you mind sending the report by tomorrow?
sentences.explanation: WOULD YOU MIND + gerund (-ing). Not 'to send' — 'sending'.

sentences.wrong: Could you possibly to help me with this?
sentences.correct: Could you possibly help me with this?
sentences.explanation: After COULD YOU, use base verb directly — no 'to'. 'Could you possibly help' not 'to help'.

sentences.wrong: Would it be possible changing the time?
sentences.correct: Would it be possible to change the time?
sentences.explanation: WOULD IT BE POSSIBLE + TO + base verb (infinitive). Not the -ing form.

content.scenario: You are in a library. Someone near you is talking loudly on the phone. It's disturbing you and others. What do you say?
options: Excuse me — would you mind taking your call outside? People are trying to study.
content.correct: Excuse me — would you mind taking your call outside? People are trying to study.
content.explanation: Public place + stranger = polite request needed. 'Excuse me' softens it further. WOULD YOU MIND + -ing is ideal.

content.instruction: Make 5 polite requests for different situations using different structures from this lesson.
content.prompt:
Create a polite request for each situation:
1. Asking a colleague to cover your work while you're on leave.
2. Asking a stranger to take your photo at a tourist spot.
3. Asking your manager for a one-day deadline extension.
4. Asking a fellow passenger to switch seats on a train.
5. Asking a customer service agent to repeat information slowly.
content.example_response: Here are my five polite requests! For the first one — asking a colleague to cover my work: 'Hey Ravi, I was wondering if you could possibly cover my tasks on Thursday while I'm on leave? I'll prepare a detailed handover note so everything is clear.' For the second — asking a stranger for a photo: 'Excuse me, would you mind taking a quick photo of us? We're trying to get the whole monument in the frame!' For the manager — deadline extension: 'I was wondering if it would be possible to have one additional day for the report? The data analysis is taking longer than I anticipated.' For the train seat — 'Excuse me, would you mind swapping seats with me? I'd prefer to sit near the window for the journey.' And for customer service: 'Could you possibly repeat that information a little more slowly? I want to make sure I note it correctly.'
key_phrases: I was wondering if you could…

content.instruction: Re-read Scenario 3 (Bank call dialogue). Then role-play: you are calling to dispute a transaction on your credit card. Use polite requests throughout.
content.prompt: Role-play a bank call in which you report an unknown charge on your card. Use: Could you please…?, Would you mind…?, I was wondering if…, Would it be possible to…?
content.example_response: Good morning! I'm calling to report an issue with my credit card. I noticed an unfamiliar transaction on my statement and I'd like to get some help with it. Could you please pull up my account? My name is Arjun Mehta and my card number ends in 4391. Yes, thank you for confirming. I was wondering if you could tell me more about a transaction of ₹3,200 from a merchant called 'QuickShop Online' dated the 12th? I don't recall making that purchase. It does look suspicious — would it be possible to raise a dispute for that transaction? I'd appreciate that. And would you mind sending me the dispute reference number by SMS once it's raised? I'd like to keep a record. Also — could you possibly block that card and issue me a replacement? I'm worried there may have been unauthorised access. How long would a replacement take to arrive? Three to five business days — that's fine. Thank you so much for your help today!

content.instruction: Complete this email using polite request language.
content.text:
Subject: Request for Support — Project Documentation

Dear Sonal,

I hope you're well. I'm working on the year-end project documentation and I ___ (1) wondering if you ___ (2) possibly share the Q3 reports with me. I understand you may be busy — ___ (3) it be possible to send them by Wednesday?

Also, ___ (4) you mind reviewing the summary I've drafted? I'd really ___ (5) a second opinion before I submit it to the manager.

Finally, ___ (6) you possibly spare 20 minutes this week for a quick catch-up call?

I ___ (7) really appreciate your help. Please do not hesitate to let me know if any of this is ___ (8) for you.

Kind regards,
Kavya

content.title: Lesson 4 complete — Polite requests
key_points: Scale: Can you? → Could you? → Could you possibly? → Would you mind? → I was wondering if...
WOULD YOU MIND + verb-ING (not 'to')
WOULD IT BE POSSIBLE + TO + base verb
'Not at all' / 'Of course' = positive response to 'Would you mind?'
COULD YOU POSSIBLY = extra polite, good for formal situations
Add 'when you have a moment' / 'if it's not too much trouble' to soften further
